text: My name is Dr. Ismanto Sukhandi. I'm a HPP surgeon at AdventHealth Tampa. It's an honor and privilege for me to present our video during SAGE's virtual meeting. This is about a robotic Roux-en-Y hepatic autogenostomy for a right hepatic duct transection during a laparoscopic olesostectomy.

The cholangiogram reveals multiple clips placed at the entrance of the right hepatic duct, with the right hepatic duct not visible, while the left hepatic system remains intact. The surgical team employs a robotic approach using five trocars.

The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions between the transverse colon and the gallbladder fossa, revealing a biloma collection in the subhepatic fossa. Clips are visible at the entrance of the right hepatic duct into the bifurcation.

The surgeon removes the PTC to facilitate liver mobilization cephalad, exposing the porta hepatis. The falciform ligament is dissected up to the hepatic confluence, and a laparoscopic liver retractor elevates the liver. The Haller plate is lowered to reveal the leaking right hepatic duct, which is cannulated with a catheter. A cholangiogram confirms the anatomy, and the Roux limb is being created.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures approximately 35 centimeters distally. A mesenteric window is created to perform a stapled side-to-side jejunojejunostomy. The omentum is split while ensuring the Roux limb is not devascularized. A robotic blue load stapler is used to create the anastomosis, and the common enterotomy is closed with a running suture using a 3-0 V-Loc suture.

The surgeon demonstrates robotic suturing techniques, starting with an anchoring stitch at the inferior corner of the common enterotomy. The edges of the bowel wall are imbricated to ensure a secure anastomosis. The robotic system facilitates precise suturing, with stitches meeting in the middle to complete the anastomosis. The bedside assistant aids in transposing the Roux limb, and a small enterotomy is created for the upcoming hepaticojejunostomy.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis at the nine o'clock position on the right hepatic duct, with the rest of the biliary system intact. The anastomosis is performed at a high level near the hilar plate, distal to the bifurcation. Using the robotic system, the surgeon places sutures in a running fashion on the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring precise placement to prevent postoperative leakage. The bedside assistant uses suction to clear leaking bile, maintaining a clear surgical view.

The surgeon uses a regular needle holder to create the anterior aspect of the anastomosis at the nine o'clock position, completing the front wall. The robotic system aids in precise needle placement, ensuring no bile leakage. An anchoring stitch is placed carefully at the hilum plate to reduce tension, avoiding injury to liver inflow structures. Another anchoring stitch is placed at the three o'clock position to suspend the Roux limb.
The surgeon places a drain using the robotic system, positioning it through a port site in the right quadrant. The surgical field is clear, with minimal estimated blood loss of fifty cc.
